Author: Philippe Ensarguet Philippe Ensarguet Published Dec
Title: Agent engineering: Rethinking how we build and integrate in the age of AI
Text:
Agent engineering: Rethinking how we build and integrate in the age of AI
This morning, during my daily tech watch, I came across an interesting and self-questioning piece from the LangChain team titled "Agent Engineering: A New Discipline." The article crystallized something I've been observing for months now: we're witnessing the emergence of an entirely new way of building software systems.
The Death of "works on my machine"
The LangChain team makes a compelling observation—the delta between "it works on my machine" and "it works in production" has become massive when building agents. Traditional software gives us predictable inputs and definable outputs. Agents? They give us neither. Users can say literally anything, and the behavioral space is wide open.
I really like the definition they brought as it’s focusing the shift we should need to manage!
Agent engineering is the iterative process of refining non-deterministic LLM systems into reliable production experiences.
It's a cycle: build, test, ship, observe, refine, repeat. But here's the paradigm shift—shipping isn't your finish line. It's how you learn.
It looks like we should adapt and accept to pioneer something a bit different, combining product thinking, engineering, and data science in ways we've never really seen before.
But here's what's questioning myself the most...
Reading this article got me thinking about a recurring issue that I have been considering for some time, which will be explored in an upcoming Unthinkable Lab session for the Telco business in early January: how much is actually changing in terms of system integration?
We have spent decades perfecting API integration patterns. Now, with agentic systems, are we just applying old wine to new bottles, or is the bottle itself fundamentally different?
I believe the answer is the latter, and the non-deterministic nature of agents forces us to rethink everything—from discovery to governance.
Discovery: From catalogs to semantic meshes
Traditional API discovery is straightforward: you browse a catalog, read documentation, and integrate. But agents don't "browse"—they reason, interpret, and decide.
This means APIs now need rich metadata layers that describe not just what they do, but:
An agent needs to understand why an API exists, not just what it does.
Design: intent over operations
Here's where it gets interesting. When designing APIs for agentic consumption, we can't just expose technical operations anymore. We need to expose business intent and outcomes.
Imagine an API that doesn't just return an error code, but engages in conversational error handling—explaining what went wrong and negotiating alternative paths forward. The API becomes a participant in problem-solving, not just a passive endpoint.
This requires:
Recommended by LinkedIn
Versioning: From mandate to conversation
Traditional versioning is top-down: "We're deprecating v1, migrate to v2 by Q3." Agents don't follow migration schedules.
In the agentic world, versioning becomes a conversation between agent and API. The API explains its evolution—what's changed, why, and what new capabilities exist. The agent adapts accordingly, sometimes using multiple versions simultaneously based on what best serves the user's intent.
It's dynamic, contextual, and frankly, a bit messy. But that's the point.
Governance: From gates to guardrails
Perhaps the most profound shift is in governance. Static access control lists and role-based permissions aren't enough when an autonomous agent is making real-time decisions that affect your business.
We need to evolve toward dynamic behavioral guardrails with:
This isn't your father's API governance framework. This is governance that operates at the speed of reasoning, not just at the speed of code.
The real challenge ahead
The LangChain team emphasizes that successful agent engineering teams have stopped trying to perfect agents before launch. Instead, they treat production as their primary teacher, systematically tracing decisions, evaluating at scale, and shipping improvements rapidly.
But I'm wondering if the challenge isn't going deeper.
We're not just learning how to build better agents—we're learning how to build entire ecosystems that support non-deterministic behavior while maintaining reliability, security, and trust.
Every API we design, every integration pattern we establish, every governance framework we implement needs to account for the fundamental reality that agents don't behave like traditional software. Every input is an edge case. "Working" isn't binary. And debugging requires inspecting reasoning, not just code.
What this means for you
If you're building agents, you already know the struggle. If you're building APIs that agents will consume, it's time to rethink your design principles. And if you're in governance, compliance, or security? Your frameworks need an upgrade for this new reality.
Agent engineering isn't a fad—it's a response to a fundamental shift in how software systems operate. The question isn't whether this discipline will become standard practice. It's how quickly your team can adapt to unlock what agents can actually do.
What are you seeing in your work? Are traditional integration patterns holding up, or are you pioneering new approaches? Let's discuss in the comments.
